question: What is Dependency Injection and why does Spring use it?
shortAnswer: DI means objects receive their dependencies from outside rather than creating them. Spring uses it to decouple components, enable testability (inject mocks), and manage object lifecycle centrally. Instead of `new EmailService()`, Spring injects the right implementation.
detailedAnswer:
Dependency Injection inverts the control of object creation. Instead of a class creating its dependencies (tight coupling), they are provided externally.

Benefits:
1. Testability — inject mocks without changing production code
2. Loose coupling — classes depend on interfaces, not implementations
3. Single Responsibility — class does not manage lifecycle of its dependencies
4. Configuration flexibility — swap implementations per environment

Spring provides DI via:
- Constructor injection (preferred — immutable, clear dependencies)
- Field injection (@Autowired on field — concise but harder to test)
- Setter injection (optional dependencies)

In Spring Boot, auto-configuration takes DI further: it automatically configures beans based on classpath dependencies.
keyPoints: Inversion of Control — dependencies provided, not created
Spring manages bean lifecycle and scope
redFlags: Cannot explain WHY DI matters (only knows HOW)
Confusing DI with Service Locator pattern

followUps.question: Constructor vs field injection — which do you prefer and why?
followUps.hint: Constructor: immutable, clear dependencies, easier to test without Spring context.

followUps.question: What happens when you have two beans of the same type?
How does DI relate to the SOLID principles?
followUps.hint: DIP — depend on abstractions. OCP — extend via new implementations.

realExperience.title: SGN3 CDI injection
realExperience.description: Used CDI (Jakarta EE equivalent of Spring DI) throughout SGN3. Field injection with @Inject for 800+ beans. Moving to constructor injection in new code for testability.

question: How does Spring Boot auto-configuration work?
shortAnswer: Spring Boot scans META-INF/spring/org.springframework.boot.autoconfigure.AutoConfiguration.imports for configuration classes. Each class has @Conditional annotations that check classpath, properties, or existing beans. If conditions match, beans are created automatically.
detailedAnswer:
Auto-configuration is Spring Boot's "opinionated defaults" mechanism:

1. Starter dependencies add JARs to classpath
2. Spring Boot scans for AutoConfiguration classes
3. @Conditional annotations check: Is H2 on classpath? Is DataSource bean missing? Is property set?
4. If conditions pass → beans are created with sensible defaults
5. You can override any auto-configured bean by defining your own

Example: spring-boot-starter-data-jpa adds Hibernate + DataSource auto-config. If you define your own DataSource bean, auto-config backs off.

Key annotations:
- @ConditionalOnClass — only if class is on classpath
- @ConditionalOnMissingBean — only if no user-defined bean exists
- @ConditionalOnProperty — only if property is set

This is why Spring Boot "just works" — you add a starter and get a working configuration.
keyPoints: Backs off when you define your own beans
redFlags: "It is magic" — cannot explain the mechanism
Does not know how to disable or override auto-configuration

followUps.question: How do you disable a specific auto-configuration?
How would you create your own auto-configuration?
followUps.hint: Create @Configuration class, add @Conditional, register in META-INF imports file.

question: Explain @Transactional propagation levels. When would you use REQUIRES_NEW?
shortAnswer: Propagation defines what happens when a transactional method calls another. REQUIRED (default) joins existing TX. REQUIRES_NEW suspends the current TX and creates a new one. I use REQUIRES_NEW when an operation must commit independently — like audit logging that must persist even if the main operation rolls back.
detailedAnswer:
Propagation levels:
- REQUIRED (default): join existing TX, or create new if none exists
- REQUIRES_NEW: suspend current TX, create fresh one. Commits/rollbacks independently.
- NESTED: savepoint within current TX (not all providers support)
- SUPPORTS: join if exists, run non-transactional if not
- NOT_SUPPORTED: suspend current TX, run non-transactional
- MANDATORY: must have existing TX, throw if none
- NEVER: throw if TX exists

When to use REQUIRES_NEW:
1. Audit logging — must persist even if business operation fails
2. Sequence/counter generation — must commit to avoid gaps
3. External notification records — track attempts independently

Pitfall: REQUIRES_NEW holds TWO connections simultaneously (outer is suspended, inner needs its own). Under high load, this can exhaust the connection pool.

Self-invocation trap: calling @Transactional method from the same class bypasses the proxy → propagation is ignored.
keyPoints: Audit logging is classic REQUIRES_NEW use case

followUps.question: What is the self-invocation problem with @Transactional?
followUps.hint: Calling @Transactional from same class skips the proxy. Solution: extract to separate bean.

followUps.question: What happens to REQUIRES_NEW if the outer transaction rolls back?
followUps.hint: Inner already committed — it is independent. This can cause inconsistency.

followUps.question: How does this compare to Jakarta EE CMT?
followUps.hint: Same concepts: REQUIRED, REQUIRES_NEW, NOT_SUPPORTED. Different mechanism (EJB interceptors vs Spring AOP).

realExperience.title: SGN3 CMT transaction management
realExperience.description: Used NOT_SUPPORTED for orchestrators calling external services (LDAP, Moodle) to avoid holding JDBC connections during network I/O. REQUIRES_NEW equivalent for audit trail persistence.

question: How do you handle errors in a Spring Boot REST API?
shortAnswer: I use @ControllerAdvice with @ExceptionHandler methods that map domain exceptions to appropriate HTTP status codes and a consistent error response DTO. Business rule violations → 409, validation errors → 400, not found → 404, unexpected → 500 with no stack trace exposed.
keyPoints: Map domain exceptions to HTTP status codes semantically
Consistent error response DTO (message, code, timestamp)
Never expose stack traces to clients
Bean Validation → collect all field errors
redFlags: Letting Spring default error page show stack traces
Catching exceptions in every controller method individually

followUps.question: How do you handle validation errors with field-level detail?
followUps.hint: MethodArgumentNotValidException → iterate FieldErrors → return map of field→message.

followUps.question: How do you prevent leaking internal details in error messages?
followUps.hint: Generic messages for 500s, specific only for client errors.

question: What is the N+1 problem and how do you solve it in Spring Data JPA?
shortAnswer: N+1 means loading a parent entity then issuing 1 query per child relationship. For 100 orders with items, that is 1 + 100 = 101 queries. Solutions: JOIN FETCH in JPQL, @EntityGraph on repository methods, batch fetching (@BatchSize), or DTOs with custom queries.
detailedAnswer:
The N+1 problem:
1. Query loads N parent entities (1 query)
2. Accessing a lazy relationship on each parent → 1 query per parent (N queries)
3. Total: N+1 queries instead of 1-2

Solutions (in order of preference):
1. JOIN FETCH in JPQL: `SELECT o FROM Order o JOIN FETCH o.items`
2. @EntityGraph: `@EntityGraph(attributePaths = {"items"})` on repository method
3. @BatchSize(size=50): Hibernate loads lazy collections in batches
4. DTO projection: custom query returning only needed fields (no entities)

Trade-offs:
- JOIN FETCH can cause cartesian product with multiple collections
- @EntityGraph is cleaner but limited (one level deep easily)
- @BatchSize is global — applies everywhere
- DTO projection is most efficient but loses entity features

Detection: enable Hibernate SQL logging in dev, use tools like p6spy, or watch slow query logs.
keyPoints: 1 + N queries instead of 1-2 optimal queries
JOIN FETCH is the standard fix for single relationships
Cartesian product risk with multiple JOIN FETCHes
DTO projections avoid the problem entirely

followUps.question: What happens if you JOIN FETCH two collections?
followUps.hint: Cartesian product — use @BatchSize for the second, or separate queries.

followUps.question: How do you detect N+1 in production?
followUps.hint: SQL logging, APM tools, Hibernate statistics, slow query logs.

realExperience.title: SGN3 N+1 detection via Glowroot
realExperience.description: Found a page doing 48,000 queries (N+1 on enrollment→student→person chain). Fixed with LATERAL JOIN and DTO projection. Response time: 48s → 2s.

question: How do you test a Spring Boot application? What is your testing strategy?
shortAnswer: Testing pyramid: unit tests (fast, no Spring context, Mockito) for business logic, integration tests (@SpringBootTest or @DataJpaTest with Testcontainers) for persistence and API, and minimal E2E tests for critical paths. I aim for 80% coverage on business logic, not on boilerplate.
detailedAnswer:
Testing strategy (pyramid):

1. Unit tests (70%) — fast, isolated:
- Service layer with mocked repositories
- Pure business logic, validation
- @ExtendWith(MockitoExtension.class)
- No Spring context needed

2. Integration tests (25%) — verify wiring:
- @DataJpaTest — JPA repositories with real DB (Testcontainers)
- @WebMvcTest — controllers with MockMvc
- @SpringBootTest — full context for critical flows

3. E2E tests (5%) — happy paths:
- Full application running
- REST API calls end-to-end
- Only critical business flows

Key practices:
- Testcontainers for real PostgreSQL (not H2)
- Test data builders (not random data)
- One assertion per concept
- Test behavior, not implementation
- @Transactional on integration tests for rollback
keyPoints: Mockito for unit tests (no Spring context needed)
Testcontainers for real database in integration tests
Test behavior and outcomes, not implementation details
redFlags: "I use H2 for tests" (misses PostgreSQL-specific behavior)
No clear strategy — tests are random
Testing implementation details (verify every method call)

followUps.question: Why Testcontainers instead of H2?
followUps.hint: H2 differs in SQL dialect, JSON support, window functions. Real DB catches real bugs.

followUps.question: How do you handle test data setup?
followUps.hint: Builders/factories, not random. Each test creates what it needs.

realExperience.title: SGN3 testing with JUnit 5 + Mockito
realExperience.description: Unit tests for business logic (900+ tests), Weld-JUnit for CDI integration. H2 for simple JPQL, real PostgreSQL for native queries and complex scenarios.

question: How do you manage configuration across different environments?
shortAnswer: Profile-based configuration (application-{profile}.yml) for environment-specific values, externalized config via environment variables for secrets (never in code), and Spring Cloud Config or Kubernetes ConfigMaps for centralized management. Sensible defaults in the base application.yml.
detailedAnswer:
Configuration hierarchy (lowest to highest priority):
1. application.yml — sensible defaults
2. application-{profile}.yml — environment overrides
3. Environment variables — secrets and deployment-specific
4. Command line arguments — emergency overrides

Principles:
- Secrets NEVER in source code (env vars, Vault, K8s secrets)
- Base config has safe defaults (timeouts, pool sizes)
- Profile activation via SPRING_PROFILES_ACTIVE env var
- 12-factor: config in environment, not code

For microservices:
- Spring Cloud Config Server for centralized config
- Kubernetes ConfigMaps/Secrets mounted as files
- Feature flags via config (not code branches)

What I configure per environment:
- Database URLs and credentials
- External service endpoints
- Log levels
- Feature flags
- Pool sizes and timeouts
keyPoints: Secrets in env vars, never in code
Spring Cloud Config or K8s ConfigMaps for centralized management
redFlags: No strategy — different config files copied manually

followUps.question: How do you handle secrets rotation without redeployment?
followUps.hint: Spring Cloud Vault with lease renewal, or K8s secrets with pod restart policy.

followUps.question: What is your strategy for feature flags?
followUps.hint: Property-driven, not code branches. Toggles per environment.

realExperience.title: SGN3 MicroProfile Config
realExperience.description: Used MicroProfile Config with properties file in WAR as defaults + environment variable overrides in OKD. Same principle as Spring profiles.

question: What is Spring Boot Actuator and how would you use it in production?
shortAnswer: Actuator exposes operational endpoints: /health (liveness/readiness), /metrics (JVM, HTTP, custom), /info, /env. In production, I expose only /health publicly for K8s probes, secure other endpoints, and integrate /metrics with Prometheus+Grafana for monitoring.
detailedAnswer:
Spring Boot Actuator provides production-ready features:

1. Health checks (/actuator/health):
- Liveness: is the app alive? (K8s restarts if not)
- Readiness: can it accept traffic? (K8s removes from load balancer)
- Custom health indicators for critical dependencies

2. Metrics (/actuator/metrics):
- JVM: heap, GC, threads
- HTTP: request count, latency percentiles
- Custom: business metrics (orders/min, enrollments/day)
- Export to Prometheus, Datadog, etc.

3. Info (/actuator/info): build version, git commit

Security:
- /health/liveness and /health/readiness → public (K8s needs them)
- Everything else → authenticated or internal network only
- Never expose /env or /heapdump publicly

Kubernetes integration:
```yaml
livenessProbe:
  httpGet: { path: /actuator/health/liveness, port: 8080 }
readinessProbe:
  httpGet: { path: /actuator/health/readiness, port: 8080 }
```
keyPoints: Health checks for K8s liveness/readiness probes
Custom health indicators for critical dependencies
redFlags: Not knowing the liveness vs readiness distinction
No custom health checks for database or external services

followUps.question: What is the difference between liveness and readiness?
followUps.hint: Liveness: should restart. Readiness: should stop sending traffic. Different failure modes.

followUps.question: How would you add a custom health check?
followUps.hint: Implement HealthIndicator, return Health.up() or Health.down() with details.

realExperience.title: SGN3 health probes in OKD
realExperience.description: Configured MicroProfile Health (equivalent to Actuator) for liveness and readiness in OKD. Readiness includes database check; liveness does not include external dependencies.
